content.body: Quantum mechanics reveals that matter also has wave properties (de Broglie). Electrons in atoms exist only in discrete energy levels — they absorb or emit photons when jumping between levels.

de Broglie wavelength: λ = h/mv = h/p

Bohr model energy levels (hydrogen):
Eₙ = -13.6/n² eV

Photon emitted: hf = E_high - E_low

content.title: Energy Level Transition
content.body:
Electron drops from n=3 to n=1 in hydrogen:
E₃ = -13.6/9 = -1.51 eV
E₁ = -13.6/1 = -13.6 eV
hf = E₃ - E₁ = -1.51-(-13.6) = 12.09 eV
This UV photon is in the Lyman series

questions.question: de Broglie's hypothesis states that matter:
questions.explanation: de Broglie proposed λ = h/mv: all matter has an associated wavelength

questions.question: In the hydrogen atom (Bohr model), the ground state is:
questions.explanation: The lowest energy level (ground state) is n=1 with E₁ = -13.6 eV

questions.question: When an electron falls to a lower energy level, it:
questions.explanation: Energy is released as a photon when an electron drops to a lower level

questions.question: The energy of hydrogen's ground state is:
questions.explanation: E₁ = -13.6/1² = -13.6 eV (negative means bound state)

questions.question: A heavier particle moving at the same speed has a de Broglie wavelength that is:
questions.explanation: λ = h/mv — larger mass → smaller wavelength

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

content.body: The development of atomic models progressed from Thomson's 'plum pudding' to Rutherford's nuclear model to Bohr's quantized model to modern quantum mechanics.

Bohr's Model for Hydrogen:
Electron energy levels: Eₙ = −13.6/n² eV
n = 1: ground state (−13.6 eV)
n = 2: first excited state (−3.4 eV)

Photon emitted when electron falls:
E = hf = E_higher − E_lower

Ionisation energy = 13.6 eV (remove electron completely)

Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle:
Δx·Δp ≥ h/4π
ΔE·Δt ≥ h/4π

Cannot simultaneously know exact position and momentum.
At quantum scale, observation affects the system.

questions.question: Bohr's model explained...
questions.explanation: Bohr's model successfully explained the line spectrum of hydrogen by quantizing electron orbits.

questions.question: Ground state of hydrogen has energy...
questions.explanation: Ground state (n=1) energy = −13.6/1² = −13.6 eV. Negative means the electron is bound to the nucleus.

questions.question: A photon is emitted when an electron...
options: Falls to a lower energy level
questions.answer: Falls to a lower energy level
questions.explanation: When an electron drops from higher to lower energy level, the energy difference is released as a photon.

questions.question: Ionisation energy of hydrogen =
questions.explanation: Ionisation energy = energy needed to remove the electron (from n=1 to n=∞) = 13.6 eV.

questions.question: Heisenberg's uncertainty principle states that...
options: Position and momentum cannot both be known precisely
questions.answer: Position and momentum cannot both be known precisely
questions.explanation: ΔxΔp ≥ h/4π. The more precisely we know position, the less precisely we can know momentum, and vice versa.

questions.question: Rutherford's experiment (gold foil) proved atoms have...
options: A dense positive nucleus with mostly empty space
questions.answer: A dense positive nucleus with mostly empty space
questions.explanation: Most alpha particles passed through (empty space), but some were deflected greatly — proving a dense, concentrated nucleus.

questions.question: Energy level n=2 in hydrogen =
A photon is emitted as electron drops from n=3 to n=1. Energy of photon (eV) ≈
Quantum mechanics replaced Bohr's model because Bohr's model...
questions.explanation: Bohr's model failed for multi-electron atoms. Quantum mechanics provides a complete treatment for all atoms.

questions.question: Wave function ψ in quantum mechanics gives...
options: Probability of finding electron at a location
questions.answer: Probability of finding electron at a location
questions.explanation: In quantum mechanics, |ψ|² gives the probability density — the likelihood of finding the electron at a given position.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
Bohr model: electrons in discrete orbits
Energy levels: E_n = -13.6/n² eV (hydrogen)
Photon emitted/absorbed: E = hf = E_i - E_f

Quantum numbers:
n: principal (1,2,3...) — energy level
l: angular (0 to n-1) — subshell (s,p,d,f)
m_l: magnetic (-l to +l) — orbital orientation
m_s: spin (±½) — electron spin

Pauli exclusion: no two electrons same set of 4 quantum numbers

content.title: Hydrogen Spectrum
content.body:
Lyman series: transitions to n=1 (UV)
Balmer series: transitions to n=2 (visible)
Paschen series: transitions to n=3 (IR)

E.g. n=3→2: E=(-13.6/9)-(-13.6/4)=−1.51+3.4=1.89eV
f=1.89×1.6×10⁻¹⁹/6.63×10⁻³⁴=4.56×10¹⁴Hz (red light, Hα)

content.title: Electron Configuration
content.body:
Subshells and max electrons:
1s(2), 2s(2), 2p(6), 3s(2), 3p(6), 3d(10)...
Sodium (Z=11): 1s²2s²2p⁶3s¹
Iron (Z=26): 1s²2s²2p⁶3s²3p⁶3d⁶4s²

questions.question: Bohr model: electron energy levels in hydrogen E_n = -13.6/n² eV. Ground state (n=1) =
questions.explanation: n=1: E=-13.6/1=-13.6eV (ground state, most bound)

questions.question: Photon emitted when electron transitions from n=3 to n=2. Energy (use E_n=-13.6/n²) =
Principal quantum number n determines
questions.explanation: n=1,2,3...: determines the main energy shell and average distance from nucleus

questions.question: Angular quantum number l=1 corresponds to which subshell?
Maximum electrons in n=2 shell
questions.explanation: n=2: l=0(2 electrons) + l=1(6 electrons) = 8 total

questions.question: Pauli exclusion principle states
options: no two electrons in atom can have identical set of all four quantum numbers
questions.answer: no two electrons in atom can have identical set of all four quantum numbers
questions.explanation: Pauli: each electron has unique quantum state (n,l,m_l,m_s) — basis of periodic table structure

questions.question: Electron configuration of Na (Z=11) ends in
questions.explanation: Na: 1s²2s²2p⁶3s¹ — single valence electron in 3s gives Na its reactivity

questions.question: Balmer series of hydrogen spectral lines appears in
questions.explanation: Balmer: n→2 transitions. Energies correspond to visible photons (red, blue-green, violet)

questions.question: Ionisation energy of hydrogen = energy to remove electron from n=1 =
questions.explanation: Ionisation: bring electron from -13.6eV to 0eV (free) = 13.6eV

questions.question: Spin quantum number m_s has values
questions.explanation: Electron spin: only two values, often called 'spin up' (+½) and 'spin down' (-½)

questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction

content.body:
Quantum numbers:
n: principal (energy level) n=1,2,3...
ℓ: angular momentum (subshell) ℓ=0,1,...,n-1  (s,p,d,f)
mₗ: magnetic quantum number -ℓ to +ℓ
mₛ: spin ±½

Pauli exclusion: no two electrons can have same 4 quantum numbers
Hund's rule: fill orbitals singly before pairing
Aufbau: fill lowest energy first
1s²2s²2p⁶3s²3p⁶4s²3d¹⁰...

content.title: Electron Configurations
content.body:
H: 1s¹
He: 1s²
Li: 1s²2s¹  [He]2s¹
C: 1s²2s²2p²  [He]2s²2p²
Ne: 1s²2s²2p⁶  [He]2s²2p⁶
Fe: [Ar]4s²3d⁶
Cu: [Ar]4s¹3d¹⁰ (exception — full d is stable)

Hund's rule: C has 2p↑ 2p↑ (not 2p↑↓ 2p)

content.title: Spectral Lines
content.body:
Energy levels: Eₙ=-13.6/n² eV (hydrogen)
Transition n=3→2: ΔE=(-13.6/9)-(-13.6/4)=1.89eV
λ=hc/ΔE = (6.63×10⁻³⁴×3×10⁸)/(1.89×1.6×10⁻¹⁹) = 656nm (red, Hα)
Balmer series: visible, n→2
Lyman series: UV, n→1
Paschen series: IR, n→3

questions.question: Principal quantum number n determines
questions.explanation: n=1,2,3: principal energy levels (shells). Higher n = higher energy, further from nucleus

questions.question: Subshells s,p,d,f correspond to ℓ =
Maximum electrons in p-subshell (ℓ=1, mₗ=-1,0,+1 × 2 spins)
questions.explanation: 3 p-orbitals × 2 electrons each = 6 electrons

questions.question: Pauli exclusion principle states
options: no two electrons in same atom have identical set of all four quantum numbers
questions.answer: no two electrons in same atom have identical set of all four quantum numbers
questions.explanation: Pauli: each electron must have unique combination of n, ℓ, mₗ, mₛ

questions.question: Hund's rule states
options: fill singly before pairing (maximise unpaired spins)
questions.answer: fill singly before pairing (maximise unpaired spins)
questions.explanation: Hund's rule: electrons occupy separate orbitals before pairing — minimises electron-electron repulsion

questions.question: Electron configuration of O (Z=8)
questions.explanation: O: 8 electrons → 1s²(2) 2s²(2) 2p⁴(4) = 8 total

questions.question: Hydrogen ground state energy is -13.6eV. Energy of n=3 level =
Photon emitted when electron drops from n=3 to n=2 in H has energy =
Balmer series emission lines are in the
questions.explanation: Balmer series: transitions to n=2. ΔE corresponds to visible light wavelengths (656nm, 486nm, 434nm)

questions.question: Why does Cu have configuration [Ar]4s¹3d¹⁰ not [Ar]4s²3d⁹?
options: 3d full (10) and 4s half-full (1) is especially stable
questions.answer: 3d full (10) and 4s half-full (1) is especially stable
questions.explanation: Fully filled d-subshell (3d¹⁰) has extra stability. Cu 'moves' one 4s electron to complete 3d shell

questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

lessons.title: Introduction to Quantum Field Theory Concepts

content.body: Quantum Field Theory (QFT) extends quantum mechanics to include special relativity and describes fundamental particles as excitations of quantum fields. It is the framework for the Standard Model of particle physics.

Standard Model fermions (matter):
Quarks: up, down, charm, strange, top, bottom
Leptons: electron, muon, tau + 3 neutrinos

Bosons (force carriers):
Photon γ: electromagnetic force
W⁺,W⁻,Z⁰: weak nuclear force
Gluon g: strong nuclear force
Higgs boson H⁰: gives mass to particles

Antiparticles: same mass, opposite charge
Annihilation: particle + antiparticle → 2 photons

content.title: Feynman Diagrams
content.body:
Represent particle interactions:
Vertex: particle emits/absorbs force carrier
Electron-electron repulsion: electron emits virtual photon, other electron absorbs it
Beta decay: d quark → u quark + W⁻ → u quark + electron + antineutrino
Virtual particles: short-lived, 'borrow' energy from uncertainty principle

questions.question: Standard Model contains how many types of quarks?
questions.explanation: Six quarks: up, down, charm, strange, top (truth), bottom (beauty)

questions.question: Force carrier for electromagnetic force is
questions.explanation: Photon γ: massless, carries electromagnetic force between charged particles

questions.question: Higgs boson is responsible for
options: giving particles their mass via Higgs field
questions.answer: giving particles their mass via Higgs field
questions.explanation: Higgs field permeates all space; particles interacting strongly with it have more mass

questions.question: Quarks are held together by
questions.explanation: Gluons carry strong nuclear force binding quarks into protons/neutrons. Never found in isolation (confinement)

questions.question: A proton contains
questions.explanation: Proton: uud (2 up + 1 down). Charge: 2/3+2/3-1/3=+1 ✓

questions.question: Antiparticle of electron is
questions.explanation: Positron: same mass as electron, opposite charge (+1). Annihilates with electron → 2 gamma rays

questions.question: Beta-minus decay at quark level: a down quark changes to
questions.explanation: d→u+W⁻. The W⁻ then decays: W⁻→e⁻+antineutrino. Net: n→p+e⁻+antineutrino

questions.question: Feynman diagrams represent
options: pictorial rules for calculating interaction probabilities
questions.answer: pictorial rules for calculating interaction probabilities
questions.explanation: Feynman diagrams: shorthand for mathematical terms in perturbation theory calculation of interaction amplitudes

questions.question: Which particle was the last to be discovered in the Standard Model?
questions.explanation: Higgs boson discovered at LHC (CERN) in 2012 — the last missing piece of Standard Model

questions.question: Grand Unified Theory (GUT) aims to
options: unify electromagnetic, weak and strong forces under one framework
questions.answer: unify electromagnetic, weak and strong forces under one framework
questions.explanation: GUT: merges electromagnetism, weak and strong forces at very high energies. Gravity not yet unified (open problem)

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Quantum Mechanics Introduction. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Quantum Mechanics Introduction concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Quantum Mechanics Introduction builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Quantum Mechanics Introduction. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Quantum Mechanics Introduction. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Quantum Mechanics Introduction include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Quantum Mechanics Introduction. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction

questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
